name: 3D Printing Hacks
description: 3D printing has gained importance in the world and is revolutionising the way things are manufactured, from body implants to building a house. It also offers greater flexibility in designing these products according to an individual╬ô├ç├ûs needs. In this module, students will acquire the essential skills and knowledge to design, print and process their own 3D printed objects. They will also learn to incorporate add-on features as well as to process their 3D printed parts to functionalise and beautify the parts. There will be hands-on practice sessions for students to develop their skills.
features: Covers 3D design, printing, prototyping, and post-processing through hands-on activities and project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in hands-on making, product design, prototyping, and creative technology.

name: Eating Well, Living Well
description:
As consuThis module aims to introduce how food can affect the well-being through different stages of human life cycle. As consumers develop sophisticated palates and become more health conscious, the food and wellness industries are evolving to produce food products with enhanced sensory appeal and better nutritional and health benefits.

In this module, students will acquire knowledge on the impact of food on health and the dietary requirements for different groups of people such as the aged and chronically sick. In addition, students will examine how technology is changing food production and consumption habits. As a result, students will become better informed consumers and learn to use food to manage physical fitness and prevent diseases.
features: Covers food, nutrition, wellness, and healthy lifestyle choices through project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in food, nutrition, wellness, and making healthier lifestyle decisions.

name: Towards a Greener Planet
description: In line with a universal call to action to end poverty, protect the planet, and ensure that by 2030 all people enjoy peace and prosperity, it is becoming increasingly important as individuals to be aware, knowledgeable and be willing to contribute to its attainment. This module introduces the concept of sustainability and its relevance in environmental, social and economic contexts. It focuses on the environmental aspect of sustainable development, and discusses current practices and trends that have positive impacts to organisations. It also explores how communities and individuals can embrace sustainability in day-to-day activities and lifestyles.
features: Covers sustainability, environmental practices, and social responsibility through project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in sustainability, environmental issues, and community impact.

name: Anatomy and Physiology
description: This module poses triggers that stimulate students to appreciate the order by which the human body is organised and helps them understand the holistic aspects of the human biological systems. It also gives students a fundamental understanding of how the various systems in the human body function together to maintain a balance. Topics covered in the module include important levels of organisation for structure and function such as cells, tissues, organs, organ systems, and the whole human body / organism; anatomical directions such as body sections, body regions and body cavities; the skeletal-muscular system for support and movement; the nervous and endocrine system for body systems communication and maintaining balance; systems that are involved in the distribution of food and oxygen namely the digestive, circulatory and respiratory systems; and the lymphatic and urinary systems to maintain and regulate body fluids. An introduction to surface anatomy is also included.
features: Covers human body systems, anatomy, physiology, and biological functions.
suitableFor: Students interested in health sciences, human biology, sports science, and healthcare pathways.

name: Biology
description: This module offers a basic foundation in biology. Students will be introduced to fundamental concepts as well as the vocabulary and practices of the field. Key topics include basic biomolecules, chemical bonding and intermolecular interactions, eukaryotic and prokaryotic cell structure, cellular division and basic genetic inheritance patterns, and evolutionary and population biology. Students will also get hands-on practice developing and testing hypotheses in research studies.
features: Covers biological concepts, cells, genetics, and scientific investigation through hands-on activities and laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in biology, life sciences, research, and scientific problem-solving.

name: General and Physical Chemistry
description: This module aims to provide the foundation that is required for students to develop an understanding of chemical principles of how atoms interact with one another to form molecules, and how these molecules interact with one another to give rise to the properties and behaviour of substances. Topics covered in this module include periodic properties of the elements, stoichiometry, chemical bonding, thermochemistry, chemical equilibrium, acid-base equilibria, electrochemistry, reaction kinetics and chemical thermodynamics. Students will be equipped with the necessary laboratory skills to perform dilution, titration and study of rate law, learn how to interpret experimental data and report experimental results.
features: Covers chemical principles, laboratory techniques, reactions, and data interpretation through laboratory practice and project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in chemistry, applied science, laboratory work, and analytical thinking.

name: Organic and Inorganic Chemistry
description:
This module aims to provide concepts in the structure, properties and reactions of inorganic compounds (metals and organometallic compounds) and organic compounds (carbon containing compounds). Topics covered in organic chemistry include the structure, IUPAC nomenclature and functional groups of organic compounds (alkanes, alkenes, alkynes, alcohols, carboxylic acids, aldehydes, ketones, esters), isomers of organic compounds, basic principles of organic reaction and reaction mechanisms such as free radical substitution, electrophilic addition and nucleophilic substitution. Students will get to learn inorganic chemistry topics such as the properties of main group metals, non-metals and transition metals, and coordination chemistry. Students would gain competence in basic laboratory techniques through practical sessions such as design and planning of experiment, neutralization, displacement, precipitation and complex ion formation.

[Module Synopsis for students in AY2024 intake and earlier] 
The module aims to provide an understanding of the concepts behind the practical applications of organic and inorganic compounds as they form one of the primary basis for chemistry. Topics covered includes the periodic table of elements, the transition elements, hydrocarbons, alkyl halides and alcohols, and carbonyl and nitrogen compounds. In this module, students will also acquire competency and attain proficiency in standard laboratory skills, interpret and evaluate observations and experimental data.
features: Covers chemical principles, laboratory techniques, reactions, and data interpretation through hands-on activities and laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in chemistry, applied science, laboratory work, and analytical thinking.

name: Physics
description: This module is designed to develop students╬ô├ç├û basic foundation in physics for further study in engineering, science, and other related fields. In this module, students will be introduced to fundamental principles and calculations in the topics of kinematics, projectile motion, momentum, force, energy, power, heat, thermodynamics, optics, mechanical properties, photons, fluid dynamics and electromagnetism through tackling and solving real life applications as well as engaging in hands-on activities.
features: Covers physics principles, calculations, real-life applications, and practical activities through hands-on activities and project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in physics, engineering, applied science, and quantitative problem-solving.

name: Laboratory Practices and Safety
description:
Within the laboratory setting, workplace safety and health issues are viewed in relation to the BizSAFE Level 2 framework.

In this module, the perspectives will focus on the motivation, skills and practices within the scientific industry, and examine compliance to national standards and regulatory bodies. Aspects of basic resource management, total quality management, good laboratory practices (GLPs) and good documentation practices (ISO standards) will be covered. Students will also be taught the fundamentals of laboratory information systems, laboratory safety, reference laboratory services, scientific proposal and report writing.
features: Covers laboratory practices, safety, documentation, quality standards, and compliance through laboratory practice and real-world applications.
suitableFor: Students interested in laboratory work, applied science operations, quality control, and safe practice.

name: Laboratory Practices and Safety
description:
This module introduces the concept of workplace safety and health practices in the laboratory, relevant to the applied science sectors, in compliance to BizSAFE Level 2 framework. 

Students will be introduced to basic laboratory testing and analytical skills, Good Laboratory Practices (GLPs) and Documentation Practices (GDPs), ISO standards and total quality management, in accordance with national standards and regulating bodies.
features: Covers laboratory practices, safety, documentation, quality standards, and compliance through laboratory practice and real-world applications.
suitableFor: Students interested in laboratory work, applied science operations, quality control, and safe practice.

name: Molecular Cell Biology and Biochemistry
description: This module provides an in-depth exploration of the cell at a molecular level. Students will study the design and function of cellular organelles, the complex architecture of proteins, nucleic acids, and other macromolecules, and their roles in key biochemical pathways. The curriculum includes a detailed analysis of metabolic processes such as glycolysis, the citric acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation, alongside the mechanisms of DNA replication, transcription, and RNA translation. It covers the dynamics of cell signalling, the molecular basis of genetic regulation, and cell cycle control. The laboratory sessions focus on enzyme kinetics, bridging the gap between theoretical knowledge and practical laboratory skills. Through this module, students will gain a holistic understanding of cellular life and its biochemical underpinnings, equipping them for further research and professional practice in biological sciences.
features: Covers biological concepts, cells, genetics, and scientific investigation through hands-on activities and laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in biology, life sciences, research, and scientific problem-solving.

name: Mathematics
description: This foundation module focuses on the essentials of mathematics that would be useful and relevant for a wide range of applications. Emphasis will be placed on the understanding of key concepts, the skills in solving problems and the use of a data computational tool. Students will learn key concepts and applications in basic algebra, sequences, functions, graphs, probability, and statistics.
features: Covers mathematical concepts, quantitative reasoning, and problem-solving techniques through problem-solving tasks.
suitableFor: Students interested in mathematics, analytics, engineering, science, and structured problem-solving.

name: Mathematics for Applied Science
description: This module introduces mathematical principles essential for applied sciences, emphasizing understanding, problem-solving, and application in algebra, geometry, trigonometry, and calculus. Key topics encompass algebra (quadratic functions, equations, surds, polynomials, binomial expansions, exponential and logarithmic functions), geometry and trigonometry (trigonometric functions, coordinate geometry, proofs), and calculus (differentiation and integration). The aim of the module is to develop thinking, reasoning, communication, and application through a mathematical approach to problem-solving. At the end of this module, students will gain the necessary mathematical skills for scientific disciplines and advanced modules in their respective diploma programmes.
features: Covers mathematical concepts, quantitative reasoning, and problem-solving techniques through project-based work and problem-solving tasks.
suitableFor: Students interested in mathematics, analytics, engineering, science, and structured problem-solving.

name: Sustainability and Climate Change
description: The module offers an in-depth exploration of sustainability principles and their intersection with climate change. Beginning with examining the science and drivers behind climate change and global warming, the module emphasises the critical importance of sustainable development. It introduces students to the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (UNSDGs) and provides a comprehensive examination of sustainability, covering Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) aspects. The curriculum includes an analysis of international agreements such as the Paris Agreement and local initiatives like the Singapore Green Plan. The module concludes with a concentrated exploration of mitigation and adaptation measures, carbon economy, encompassing business sustainability, fostering private and community partnerships, impact on the community at large, and the circular economy. Throughout, the curriculum underscores the interconnectedness of these topics, fostering a holistic understanding and equipping students to address the urgent challenges climate change poses through informed and actionable solutions, ensuring learners acquire a comprehensive perspective on sustainability.
features: Covers sustainability, environmental practices, and social responsibility through project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in sustainability, environmental issues, and community impact.

name: Epidemiology and Biostatistics
description: Students will study public health concerns, focusing on disease and injury control within communities. The module covers the different types of epidemiologic studies, as well as surveillance techniques and methods of investigation. Innovative laboratory methods will be explored. Students will also gain essential statistical skills such as such as t-test, ANOVA, linear regression and Pearson rank correlation to organise and analyse biological data.
features: Covers data analysis, digital tools, evidence-based insights, and decision support through laboratory practice and problem-solving tasks.
suitableFor: Students interested in data, analytics, technology, research, and evidence-based decision-making.

name: Marine Ecology and Conservation
description:
This module provides a global overview of the exploitation of living marine resources and marine biological approaches to conservation. This includes marine biodiversity, critical habitats, environmental threats, fisheries exploitation, coastal zone management, and marine protected areas. The module will take a holistic approach to marine and coastal conservation, considering the economic, environmental, political, and cultural influences on marine resource management.

Students will learn about the current state of marine ecosystems and the major threats that they face, which include fisheries, pollution, diseases, and habitat loss. Students will critically review national and international marine and coastal conservation strategies and evaluate their effectiveness.
features: Covers sustainability, environmental practices, and social responsibility.
suitableFor: Students interested in sustainability, environmental issues, and community impact.

name: Protein Technologies
description:
This module delves into the structure-function relationship of proteins and other biomolecules (mainly DNA), the forces influencing the regulatory, structural, protective and transport roles of proteins. Students will acquire knowledge and skills that will allow them to be competent in applying biochemical techniques in the expression, separation, identification, and purification of proteins. Techniques in protein structure-function analysis such as SDS PAGE, column chromatography and protein crystallography will be covered in this module. In addition, students will demonstrate their understanding of protein interactions with other biomolecules using protein visualisation software. Through understanding protein interactions, students will relate to innovations in biotechnology such as drug discovery, diagnostics, and development of new therapeutic strategies. Furthermore, this module will explore the critical role of protein engineering and design in within the field of synthetic biology.

[Module Synopsis for students in AY2024 intake and earlier] 
This module delves into the structure-function relationship of proteins and other biomolecules (mainly DNA), the forces influencing the regulatory, structural, protective and transport roles of proteins. Students will acquire knowledge and skills that will allow them to be competent in applying biochemical techniques in the expression, separation, identification and purification of proteins. Techniques in protein structure-function analysis such as SDS PAGE, column chromatography and protein crystallography will be covered in this module. In addition, students will demonstrate their understanding of protein interactions with other biomolecules through protein visualisation software.
features: Covers biological concepts, cells, genetics, and scientific investigation through hands-on activities and project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in biology, life sciences, research, and scientific problem-solving.

name: Introduction to Agro-science
description: Students will study the fundamentals of agriculture in this module. This includes the biology of plant growth and other factors influencing the development of agricultural produce, such as soil, water and nutrient requirements. Students will also learn about the control of plant diseases and pest management on farms.
features: Covers biological concepts, cells, genetics, and scientific investigation through project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in biology, life sciences, research, and scientific problem-solving.

name: Plant Genetics and Tissue Culture
description: Through this module, students will gather knowledge on the basics, methodology and applications of plant tissue culture and genetic transformation in science, agriculture and industry. The students will learn about plant reproduction, breeding techniques, polyploidy genetics and transgenic plants. The course will cover theory and practice of plant tissue culture in carrying out genetic modifications, micropropagation of endangered species and commercial varieties of plants. The module will also look into benefits and risks of GMO, Regulatory approval of GMOs and IP protection in the field of plant biotechnology.
features: Covers biological concepts, cells, genetics, and scientific investigation through real-world applications.
suitableFor: Students interested in biology, life sciences, research, and scientific problem-solving.

name: Agricultural Technology
description: Agricultural technology, or AgriTech, is the focus of this module, which aims to enhance students' understanding of using technology in agriculture to improve efficiency, sustainability, and productivity. The curriculum emphasizes knowledge of various agricultural cultivation systems, including both indoor and outdoor approaches, with a special focus on urban farming technologies like hydroponics, aeroponics, and aquaponics. Students will explore the benefits and risks associated with each system and learn about factors influencing yield improvement such as lighting, irrigation, and microclimate control. Integrated pest management, a key aspect of farm operations, is also covered. The module highlights technological advancements in agriculture, introducing tools like Controlled-Environment Agriculture (CEA), Internet-of-Things (IoT), and automation. By understanding the critical factors in agricultural systems, students will be equipped to effectively manage an urban farm and respond to the diverse and growing demands in agriculture.
features: Covers sustainability, environmental practices, and social responsibility.
suitableFor: Students interested in sustainability, environmental issues, and community impact.

name: Polymer Chemistry
description: This module aims to provide students with knowledge of synthesis and characterisation of polymer molecules. Students will learn theΓö¼├ístructures, properties and applications of various commodity and engineering polymers.Γö¼├íThe moduleΓö¼├íalsoΓö¼├ícovers specialty polymers, reaction mechanisms such asΓö¼├ístep growth, chain growthΓö¼├ípolymerisation, copolymerisationΓö¼├íand variousΓö¼├ípolymerisationΓö¼├íprocesses.Γö¼├íIn addition, students willΓö¼├íbe equipped with theΓö¼├íessential skills and knowledge inΓö¼├íthe polymeric material characterisation such asΓö¼├ímechanical, thermal and rheological properties, and polymer molecular weight determination.
features: Covers chemical principles, laboratory techniques, reactions, and data interpretation.
suitableFor: Students interested in chemistry, applied science, laboratory work, and analytical thinking.

name: Laboratory Skills in Analytical Testing
description: This module aims to equip students with the practical skills critical for analytical chemistry testing and materials characterisation. Students will go through the full process of analytical testing from sample and reagent preparation, to operation and calibration of the equipment and results interpretation. Students will experience different analytical and materials characterisation techniques commonly used in various industries. Topics include sample and reagent preparation and treatment, chromatographic and spectrometric techniques, practical skills of rheological studies, mechanical characterisation, and microscopy. In addition, it allows students to pick up important troubleshooting skills to solve instrument technical issues.
features: Covers laboratory practices, safety, documentation, quality standards, and compliance through hands-on activities and laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in laboratory work, applied science operations, quality control, and safe practice.

name: Quality Assurance and Data Analytics
description:
Through this module, students will gain a fundamental understanding of quality assurance concepts as well as the statistical skills to describe, summarise and compare data. Students will learn how to use Microsoft Excel, statistical tools and data analytics software to effectively process and present data on interactive graphics. Topics covered include fundamental tools of quality control, design of experiment, data mining, descriptive and inferential statistics, regression, correlation analysis and data visualisation.

A218 Quality Assurance and Data Science will be renamed to A218 Quality Assurance and Data Analytics with effect from AY2025 intake
features: Covers data analysis, digital tools, evidence-based insights, and decision support through hands-on activities and laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in data, analytics, technology, research, and evidence-based decision-making.

name: Speciality Chemicals
description: This module introduces students to selected types of specialty chemicals (e.g. dispersants, surface active agents, pigments and dyes, plastic additives etc.) with emphasis on their chemistry, properties and functions. The applications of the specialty chemicals in various industries such as coatings, paints, lubricants, consumer care and others will be covered.
features: Covers chemical principles, laboratory techniques, reactions, and data interpretation.
suitableFor: Students interested in chemistry, applied science, laboratory work, and analytical thinking.

name: Systematics and Biodiversity
description:
Systematics and Biodiversity will equip students with a foundational understanding on species diversification, phylogenetic relationships and the biological classification of organisms. This module will explore key diagnostic characteristics of similarities and differences in organisms ranging from prokaryotes such as archaea and eubacteria to eukaryotes such as protozoa, plants, invertebrates, and vertebrates. Students will learn the rules of binomial nomenclature in taxonomy.

At the end of the course, students will gain competence in distinguishing different taxonomic groupings. For instance, students will be able to differentiate between archaea and eubacteria prokaryotes, identify clades of protozoans based on morphological traits and distinguish clades of metazoans based on characteristic differences in symmetry, tissue organisation, coelomic, and embryonic development.
features: Covers biodiversity, local wildlife, plant life, geography, and ecological patterns through project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in nature, biodiversity, environmental awareness, and local ecosystems.

name: Microbiology
description:
This module explores the unseen world of microorganisms, offering students a foundational understanding of organisms invisible to the naked eye. The curriculum covers the diversity of microorganisms, including bacteria, viruses, fungi, and protozoa, and examines their structural and functional relationships. Students will learn about the importance of microorganisms in areas such as disease processes, food processing and safety, as well as their potential for exploitation in industrial applications. Practical components are designed to complement theoretical knowledge, focusing on important techniques such as the preparation of growth media, inoculation, isolation of bacterial cultures, bacterial enumeration, and basic microbial staining for identification. In addition, the curriculum will focus on microbial nutrition, bacterial growth phases and microbial control measures such as disinfection and sterilisation methods. This module not only introduces students to the fundamentals of microbiology, but also prepares them for further applications in the field of biological sciences.

[Module Synopsis for students in AY2024 intake and earlier] 
Microbiology is a fascinating field of study, delving into a whole new world of organisms that are too small to be seen with the naked eye. This course is designed to introduce students to the diverse world of microorganisms. Structural and functional relationships will be discussed with particular reference to the role that microorganisms play in environmental cycles, disease and how they can be exploited in industrial applications. Practical components of this module, which allows students to apply the concepts covered in the theory component, will focus on basic microbiological techniques such as the preparation of growth media, inoculation and isolation of bacterial cultures, bacterial enumeration and basic microbial staining for identification. In addition, the various phases of bacterial growth will also be studied and followed.
features: Covers biological concepts, cells, genetics, and scientific investigation through hands-on activities.
suitableFor: Students interested in biology, life sciences, research, and scientific problem-solving.

name: Molecular and Cell Biology
description: This module is a core discipline module for all SAS students. It aims to help students develop an appreciation and understanding of life and life processes at the molecular and cellular level. Students explore the structure and function of the DNA molecule, and of cellular structures and processes. Critical foundations for biological subjects such as an understanding of the genetic code and the central dogma are also covered. Students should be able to communicate their understanding via presentations of their solutions for given problems, as well as through quizzes and tests. Students are expected to show how they have reasoned out their solutions, rather than simply reiterating content knowledge.
features: Covers biological concepts, cells, genetics, and scientific investigation through project-based work and problem-solving tasks.
suitableFor: Students interested in biology, life sciences, research, and scientific problem-solving.

name: Biochemistry
description: This module covers the basic principles of metabolism in living things including cellular respiration, energy-coupling reactions, protein structure and function, enzyme kinetics, fuel utilisation during starvation, regulation of biochemical processes and protection against oxidative damage and toxic substances. This is a concept-driven course that provides understanding of how living organisms harness chemical reactions for their metabolic needs. The practical component of the module introduces students to basic biochemistry laboratory experimental techniques. Concepts learnt in this module provide a foundation for other biology-related modules, for example Immunology, Microbiology, Molecular and Cell Biology, Recombinant DNA Technologies, Proteomics, Nutrition, Food Science, Structural Biology, Clinical Chemistry, Pharmaceutical Chemistry, and Pharmacology and Toxicology.
features: Covers chemical principles, laboratory techniques, reactions, and data interpretation through hands-on activities and laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in chemistry, applied science, laboratory work, and analytical thinking.

name: Recombinant DNA Technologies
description: This module enables students to acquire basic skills in molecular cloning for the production of recombinant proteins. It also provides the prerequisite exposure to basic recombinant DNA (rDNA) techniques, as well as reinforcing concepts of scientific thinking, planning of experimental work, analysis of laboratory data, record keeping, literature reading, and presentation of results. In the process, students are required to consolidate their understanding in microbiology, genetics and cell and molecular biology. Students also learn to appreciate the implications and impact of rDNA in various biotechnology-related industries, the environment and society. Topics covered in this module include issues associated with cloning such as properties of nucleic acids, DNA isolation, Polymerase Chain Reaction and agarose gel electrophoresis, electrophoresis, designing of primers, introduction of DNA into cells, and properties of restriction enzymes, among others. DNA sequencing and protein expression are also covered.
features: Covers sustainability, environmental practices, and social responsibility through hands-on activities and laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in sustainability, environmental issues, and community impact.

name: Earth and Climate Science
description: The module begins with an overview of the earth and its component systems (atmosphere, hydrosphere, and lithosphere) with emphasis on geographic and geological processes (plate tectonics and weathering). Students will then examine the impacts of anthropogenic climate change on the natural environment and explore how society and existing technologies can help deal with them.
features: Covers sustainability, environmental practices, and social responsibility.
suitableFor: Students interested in sustainability, environmental issues, and community impact.

name: Wildlife Management and Conservation
description: Focusing on meaningful conservation of wildlife populations, students will evaluate the factors that necessitate wildlife control measures as well as the challenges of resolving human-wildlife conflicts. In this module, students will also learn how integrative, science-based management approaches help to achieve a balance between sustainable development and conservation needs.
features: Covers biodiversity, local wildlife, plant life, geography, and ecological patterns through project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in nature, biodiversity, environmental awareness, and local ecosystems.

name: Terrestrial and Freshwater Ecology
description:
This module introduces students to terrestrial environments as well as lotic and lentic freshwater systems and their associated organisms. Students will explore the fundamental principles in terrestrial and freshwater ecology with a range of topics divided into three main themes:

(1) plant and animal interactions with their environment
(2) freshwater ecosystems
(3) adaptations of aquatic organisms
features: Covers sustainability, environmental practices, and social responsibility.
suitableFor: Students interested in sustainability, environmental issues, and community impact.

name: Sustainable Reporting and Communications
description:
The module introduces the concept of sustainability and its application on both the local and global scales. Students will learn about the relevance of sustainability to businesses in the modern era and the importance of communicating sustainability efforts through sustainability reporting.

Global Reporting Initiatives (GRI) Standards for sustainability reporting will also be introduced as a reference for students to apply and write a sustainability report for organisations. Students will also learn how to apply theories and principles of communication and ensure sustainability messages, as well as ideas, can be effectively conveyed within and outside organisations.
features: Covers sustainability, environmental practices, and social responsibility.
suitableFor: Students interested in sustainability, environmental issues, and community impact.

name: Marine Biology
description:
This module provides students with the fundamental knowledge of the trade-offs marine organisms experience when adapting to their environment. Students will explore the limitations that the physical environment imposes, as well as the evolutionary and physio-behavioural constraints faced by marine organisms.

Students will also learn how these constraints shape the marine organisms╬ô├ç├û life history, body form, communication, locomotive, foraging, and predator avoidance strategies. At the end of the module, students would be able to explain the behavioural and anatomical adaptive traits of major marine taxa in relation to their environment.
features: Covers biological concepts, cells, genetics, and scientific investigation.
suitableFor: Students interested in biology, life sciences, research, and scientific problem-solving.

name: Data Analytics
description: Data Analytics equips students with a foundational understanding of data analysis, emphasizing data collection techniques, statistical analysis, and data visualization using tools like Microsoft Excel. Key topics include data collection (sampling techniques, data extraction), data presentation (effective chart usage), probability (Bayes Theorem), and statistics fundamentals (central limit theorem, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, correlation). The module covers statistical models (t-test, chi-square, ANOVA) in Excel and introduces basic automation and AI for data sourcing. Students engage in hands-on data analysis and visualization to derive insights for informed decision-making. The module aims to cultivate knowledge, skills and attitude in data processing, statistical reasoning, and visualization, empowering students to make scientifically sound deductions from collected data, fostering a data-driven mindset.
features: Covers data analysis, digital tools, evidence-based insights, and decision support through hands-on activities and laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in data, analytics, technology, research, and evidence-based decision-making.

name: Immunology
description:
The study of immunology allows students to understand the cellular and molecular mechanisms governing our body's defense systems. This module seeks to provide students with a sound understanding of the immune responses which underlie clinically important areas, namely defense against infections, hypersensitivity states and allergy, immunopathology, and transplantation. Students will learn how innate and adaptive immunity synergise in providing immune defenses. Topics covered will include immunological events such as initiation of the immune response, leading from antigen presentation, cell activation and signaling pathways. Building blocks of the immune system, such as the types of cells, organs, and the major receptor molecules, including antibodies, T cell receptors and MHC molecules will be included. Contemporary challenges such as emerging infectious diseases, vaccine development, and the role of immunity in disease conditions will also be discussed. In terms of laboratory competencies, students will be trained in techniques such as SDS-PAGE, ELISA, and Western blotting.

[Module Synopsis for students in AY2024 intake and earlier] 
A231 Immunology seeks to provide students with a sound understanding of the immune responses which underlie clinically important areas, namely defense against infections, hypersensitivity states and allergy, immunopathology and transplantation. Students completing this module will gain an overview of the entire immune system, viz. Innate and Adaptive immunity. They will learn to appreciate fundamental immunological events such as initiation of the immune response, leading from antigen presentation and co-stimulation through cell activation pathways to the actions of cytokines. The module also covers the building blocks of the immune system ╬ô├ç├┤ cells, organs and the major receptor molecules, including antibodies, T cell receptors and MHC molecules. The module also seeks to enhance their skills in experimental design, conduct simple immunology experiments and keeping of laboratory records.
features: Covers patient care, healthcare settings, communication, and professional practice through hands-on activities and laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in healthcare, patient support, clinical environments, and people-centred service.

name: Genetics
description: This module focuses on the concept of genes as the molecule of heredity and DNA as the genetic material. It introduces the following basic concepts in this extremely broad subject: chromosome organisation, germline and somatic cell DNA transmission, Mendelian inheritance and phenotypic expression of genes, breeding and genetic crossing, and heritable genetic disorders and their implications. The main learning objectives of this module are to allow students to (1) show an appreciation for genetic inheritance and transmission; (2) understand the mechanisms behind genetic transmission and variability; and (3) be able to relate genetic defects to common genetic disorders and birth abnormalities.
features: Covers biological concepts, cells, genetics, and scientific investigation.
suitableFor: Students interested in biology, life sciences, research, and scientific problem-solving.

name: Pharmacology and Toxicology
description:
The basic principles of pharmacology and toxicology are covered in this concept-driven module. An integration of biology and chemistry, it offers an outline of drug actions on the body, from the autonomic and central nervous systems to the cardiovascular, respiratory, gastrointestinal, renal and endocrine systems. Students will learn the essentials of cell biology, anatomy, biochemistry, neurochemistry and physiology.

The pharmacological aspect of the course introduces the theories of pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, pharmacogenomics and molecular pharmacology, while the toxicological aspect includes toxicity testing, drug overdose, teratogenicity and drug abuse.

Students will be familiarised with the materials, equipment and laboratory techniques, such as organ baths and pharmacokinetic studies which are commonly used in pharmacology labs.
features: Covers human body systems, anatomy, physiology, and biological functions through laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in health sciences, human biology, sports science, and healthcare pathways.

name: Genetics and Fish Breeding
description: The module provides a framework for the use of genetic resources in fish production and selected approaches to change those genetic resources. Students will gain insights on the role of genetics as a component of production systems. Theoretical concepts on the various methods widely used to optimise the use of genetic population differences, individual fish, genomic differences and combinations of genetic populations (through mating) in production systems is focused. Specifically the students will be exposed to the various factors taken into account for optimising genetic improvement strategies over time, considering genomic and quantitative information available.
features: Covers biological concepts, cells, genetics, and scientific investigation.
suitableFor: Students interested in biology, life sciences, research, and scientific problem-solving.

name: Cell Culture
description:
This module provides comprehensive training in several techniques used in eukaryote cell culture. Students will understand the use of different cell culture techniques and explore their applications in different fields of research and healthcare. Students are given hands-on experience in the basic techniques of in-vitro mammalian cell culture, proliferation, fluorescence microscopy, flow cytometry, cryopreservation, gene expression through transfection procedures, and isolation of primary cell cultures. In addition, the module introduces the use of animal and plant cells as a means of producing proteins and bio-active compounds of commercial importance. Trends in the applications of cell culture such as bioprinting, cultivated meats and alternative proteins will be discussed. Through this module, students will understand how different techniques can be used to study the biology of the cell, and how they can be used in combination to answer specific questions relating to research in cell functions and their applications in life science industries.

[Module Synopsis for students in AY2024 intake and earlier] 
This module provides a theoretical introduction and direct practical training in several techniques used in eukaryote cell biology. It allows students to appreciate the holistic aspects of cell culture. It poses triggers that will stimulate students to understand the use of different cell culture techniques and to appreciate their application in different fields of research and healthcare. Students are given hands-on experience in the basic techniques of in-vitro mammalian cell culture, proliferation, fluorescence microscopy, cryopreservation, gene expression through transfection procedures, and isolation of primary cell cultures. After going through the module, students will gain an appreciation of how different techniques can be used to study the biology of the cell, and how they can be used in combination to answer specific questions relating to research in cell functions and their extended use in the pharmaceutical industry.
features: Covers biological concepts, cells, genetics, and scientific investigation through hands-on activities and real-world applications.
suitableFor: Students interested in biology, life sciences, research, and scientific problem-solving.

name: Techniques in Molecular Biology
description: This module provides an overview of the study of cells and molecules in biomedical science. Students will learn foundational skills required for biomedical research, including techniques used in genetic studies. In addition, students will be equipped with bioinformatics tools for experimental design and molecular data analysis, and be trained on the use of essential laboratory equipment.
features: Covers biological concepts, cells, genetics, and scientific investigation through hands-on activities and laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in biology, life sciences, research, and scientific problem-solving.

name: Agri-science and Tissue Culture
description: This module provides an in-depth exploration of agricultural produce growth and development, integrated with plant tissue culture techniques. Students begin by learning about plant cell organization, tissue and organ interactions. Concepts such as plant reproduction, breeding techniques, and transgenic plant studies will be covered. Emphasizing on plant nutrition, the module covers essential nutrient requirements and the significance of soil and water. Advanced topics include genetic modification, focusing on plant tissue differentiation, regeneration, and transformation. This module combines theoretical knowledge with practical laboratory skills, particularly in aseptic culture techniques, essential for plant tissue culture labs. It also addresses the regulatory aspects of genetically modified organisms (GMOs) and intellectual property in plant biotechnology. In overall, this module strives to equip students with the skills to harness tissue culture as a tool for addressing challenges in modern agriculture and ensuring global food security.
features: Covers food, nutrition, wellness, and healthy lifestyle choices through hands-on activities and laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in food, nutrition, wellness, and making healthier lifestyle decisions.

name: Microbial and Cellular Applications in Food and Health
description: Microbial and cellular sciences are essential in various industries like food, biopharmaceuticals, and agriculture. This module provides students with insights on how microorganisms and cells are used in life science research. Essential laboratory skills such as microbial identification and mammalian cell culture techniques form a core part of the curriculum. The module also covers the application of microbial analysis in food and health sectors, including methods for shelf-life testing of food products and pathogen detection. Furthermore, students explore the role of microorganisms and cells in processes like fermentation, the production of bioactive compounds and enzymes. An integral component of the module is training in basic food hygiene, culminating in a national Workforce Skills Qualification (WSQ) Food Safety Level 1 certification. Utilising laboratory and case-based learning, the curriculum aims to equip students with the skills in tackling real-world challenges in food safety, quality, nutrition, and health.
features: Covers food, nutrition, wellness, and healthy lifestyle choices through laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in food, nutrition, wellness, and making healthier lifestyle decisions.

name: Recombinant DNA Technologies
description: This module aims to help students in their acquisition of skills in molecular cloning to produce recombinant proteins. The practical-based module will focus on recombinant DNA techniques, as well as reinforce concepts of scientific thinking, planning and analysis of experiments, record keeping, literature reading, and presentation of results. Topics covered in this module will aid in the understanding of cloning, which involves knowledge in the properties of nucleic acids, DNA isolation, Polymerase Chain Reaction, agarose gel electrophoresis, designing of primers, introduction of DNA into cells, properties of restriction enzymes, and many more. Vector properties, as well as protein expression and purification will be covered as well. Throughout the module, students will be required to consolidate their prior knowledge in microbiology, genetics, and molecular cell biology.
features: Covers biological concepts, cells, genetics, and scientific investigation through hands-on activities and laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in biology, life sciences, research, and scientific problem-solving.

name: Epidemiology and Public Health
description:
Students will study public health concerns, focusing on disease and injury control within communities. The module covers the different types of epidemiologic studies, as well as surveillance techniques and methods of investigation of disease outbreak. 

It also includes health promotion strategies and techniques along with the implementations to motivate behavioural change and to plan, organise and deliver public health services. Current trends in epidemiology such as genetic, molecular and environmental aspects will also be explored. Students will learn how to perform statistical analysis to interpret epidemiological data.
features: Covers sustainability, environmental practices, and social responsibility through problem-solving tasks.
suitableFor: Students interested in sustainability, environmental issues, and community impact.

name: Environmental Testing and Analysis
description: Data collection is an important aspect of environmental monitoring and assessment. In this module, students will gain hands-on experience in performing onsite sampling and measurement of water quality parameters, detection of contaminants in water and indoor air quality audit. Principles of epidemiology and environmental health surveillance will also be discussed. Students will also be taught the proper methods to analyse and present environmental data by using visualisation software. In particular, Geographic Information Systems (GIS) - a computer-based tool that stores, manipulates and analyse geographic information, and its application in the environmental industry will be covered in this module.
features: Covers sustainability, environmental practices, and social responsibility through hands-on activities and laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in sustainability, environmental issues, and community impact.

name: Marine Biology
description: This module aims to provide students with a foundation in the basic anatomy and physiological processes in marine animals. Students will explore the major marine taxa and learn about their life-history, body forms, locomotion, foraging and predator avoidance strategies. They will also learn about the mechanisms involved in endocrine feedback and metabolism, nitrogenous waste regulation and thermoregulation. At the end of the module, students would be able to explain the behavioural, anatomical, and physiological adaptive traits of major marine taxa in relation to their environment.
features: Covers biological concepts, cells, genetics, and scientific investigation.
suitableFor: Students interested in biology, life sciences, research, and scientific problem-solving.

name: Sustainability Reporting and Carbon Management
description: This module introduces the concept of sustainability and its application in both the local and global scale. Students will learn about the relevance of sustainability to businesses in present times, and the importance of communicating sustainability efforts through sustainability reporting based on Global Reporting Initiatives (GRI) and other reporting frameworks. Students will also be introduced to fundamental concepts in carbon accounting and carbon markets. Students will be introduced to essential frameworks and guidelines to target net zero emission, including greenhouse gas protocol for carbon emission (Scopes 1, 2 and 3) and learn about decarbonisation strategies and tools.
features: Covers sustainability, environmental practices, and social responsibility.
suitableFor: Students interested in sustainability, environmental issues, and community impact.

name: Terrestrial Ecology and Wildlife Management
description: This module provides students with a foundational understanding of terrestrial and freshwater ecology, biodiversity conservation, and wildlife management. Drawing and building upon previously learnt ecological concepts, students will delve into population dynamics, community ecology, and ecosystem functions within terrestrial and freshwater environments. Students will critically assess various monitoring techniques and management strategies essential for safeguarding biodiversity, with particular emphasis on wildlife management measures and the challenges associated with human-wildlife conflicts. Subsequently, students will gain insights into the multifaceted nature of environmental conservation and develop practical skills to actively contribute to its positive advancement.
features: Covers sustainability, environmental practices, and social responsibility through hands-on activities and project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in sustainability, environmental issues, and community impact.

name: Medical Laboratory Management
description:
The A251 Medical Technology module will cover the principles and techniques of medical laboratory investigations which aid clinicians in the diagnosis of diseases and monitoring the progress of treatments. Students will learn about specimen handling, as well as the fundamental concepts of laboratory equipment used in routine clinical laboratories.  

This module provides students with an overview of the daily routine and responsibilities of a Medical Laboratory Technician, as well as the functions of the various specialized disciplines (e.g. Haematology, Clinical Chemistry, Medical Microbiology, Histopathology and Molecular Diagnostics) within a Clinical Laboratory. Students will also be introduced to Good Laboratory Practices (GLP) and familiarized with record keeping, quality control & quality assurance, literature reading, specimen analysis, methods comparisons, results interpretation and presentation. 

A251 Medical Technology will be renamed to A251 Medical Laboratory Management with effect from AY2025 intake
features: Covers laboratory practices, safety, documentation, quality standards, and compliance through laboratory practice and problem-solving tasks.
suitableFor: Students interested in laboratory work, applied science operations, quality control, and safe practice.

name: Patient Care
description: This module introduces students to the work of health professionals. Students will acquire a systematic understanding of the ways in which patients adjust to their illnesses based on the diagnosis. Students will also be taught the underlying theoretical aspects of the concept of communication in patient care, while recognising the importance of working in a multidisciplinary team of healthcare professionals. This module also provides an overview of the organisation of the hospital departments, electronic medical records system and interviewing skills to record information from patients. Equipped with essential patient care principles and skills, students will be able to appreciate and apply the attitudinal and behavioural aspects appropriate to health professionals.
features: Covers patient care, healthcare settings, communication, and professional practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in healthcare, patient support, clinical environments, and people-centred service.

name: 3D Design and Printing
description: This module provides an introduction to 3D modelling and printing. Students will learn about the design considerations for 3D printing and how to use computer aided design (CAD) software to draw models suitable for 3D printing.  Through a project, students will acquire the skills and knowledge to design and print their own 3D printed product using Fuse Deposition Modelling (FDM) printers. They will also learn to incorporate add-on features as well as to process their 3D printed parts to functionalise and beautify the parts.
features: Covers creative production, design thinking, media tools, and visual communication through hands-on activities and project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in design, media, storytelling, creative production, and visual expression.

name: Forensic Science
description: This module introduces you to the scientific principles and techniques of forensic chemistry and materials failure analysis and how they are applied to solve legal problems. The module will introduce the types and sources of forensic evidence, the general steps of forensic analysis, the microscale techniques and common laboratory equipment used for chemical analysis, and the statistical methods and quality assurance procedures used for interpretation and evaluation of results. The module will also explore the causes and mechanisms of material failure, such as corrosion, and how they can be detected and analyzed using different analytical techniques, such as microscopy, spectroscopy, chromatography, etc. The module will provide students with practical skills in performing chemical and mechanical analysis on forensic samples, such as fingerprints, drugs, metals, ceramics, polymers, etc., and communicating the findings in a clear and concise manner.
features: Covers cybersecurity, networks, digital risks, and protection strategies through hands-on activities and laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in cybersecurity, IT infrastructure, digital defence, and technical investigation.

name: Laboratory Skills in Chemistry
description: This module aims to equip students with the essential laboratory skills and techniques for chemistry, such as measuring, separating, preparing, analyzing, synthesizing, and identifying substances. It also covers the basic concepts and principles of chemistry that underlie these skills, such as stoichiometry, equilibrium, kinetics, and organic chemistry. The module also exposes students to the applications of chemistry in everyday life and the ethical and safety issues related to chemical experiments.
features: Covers chemical principles, laboratory techniques, reactions, and data interpretation through laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in chemistry, applied science, laboratory work, and analytical thinking.

name: Clinical Pharmacology and Pharmacotherapy
description: Clinical Pharmacology and Pharmacotherapy aims toΓö¼├ícombine pharmacological concepts and therapeutic principles, focusing on the clinical practices in managing cardiovascular, chronic respiratory, endocrine and infectious conditions. Students will gain knowledge of disease conditions, drug classes, mechanisms of action, and clinical applications of relevant drugs. Through applying their learning to practical patient care scenarios, students will learn to bridge the gap between theoretical knowledge and real-world practice, fostering the acquisition of the skills and attitudes essential for effective pharmacotherapy.
features: Covers patient care, healthcare settings, communication, and professional practice through hands-on activities and real-world applications.
suitableFor: Students interested in healthcare, patient support, clinical environments, and people-centred service.

name: Good Dispensing Practice
description: The Good Dispensing Practice module aims to provide a foundational understanding of industry best practices pertaining to medication safety. It is designed to equip students with the essential knowledge and skills required for accurately dispensing medications, ensuring patient safety and treatment efficacy. Through this module, students will explore medical terminology and pharmaceutical calculation concepts to accurately determining medication dosages. Additionally, students will develop the skills to utilise drug information sources to modify dosages for patients with renal and hepatic impairments and to calculate doses tailored to the needs of diverse patient populations.
features: Covers patient care, healthcare settings, communication, and professional practice through hands-on activities and project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in healthcare, patient support, clinical environments, and people-centred service.

name: Healthcare Regulations and Ethics
description: Healthcare Regulations and Ethics aims to familiarize students with the regulatory landscape governing pharmacy services and health products, pharmacy legislation, professional ethics and clinical research ethics. This module equips students with the knowledge and understanding of healthcare regulations and ethical principles essential for navigating complex healthcare environments and promoting pharmaceutical care.
features: Covers patient care, healthcare settings, communication, and professional practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in healthcare, patient support, clinical environments, and people-centred service.

name: Integrative Medicine and Nutrition
description: Integrative Medicine and Nutrition provides students with a holistic understanding of the integration of conventional medicine with complementary therapies and nutrition. This module explores the principles, practices, and evidence-based approaches to promote health and wellness. Students will gain insights into traditionalΓö¼├ímedicine, acupuncture, chiropractic care, physiotherapy and nutrition, recognizing their impact on overall health and specific medical conditions. Students will learn to critically evaluate the scientific evidence behind integrative practices and the use of dietary interventions for managing and preventing diseases. Students will also explore ethical and cultural issues surrounding complementary integrative medicine practices, as well as safety considerations, contraindications, and potential interactions with conventional treatments.
features: Covers food, nutrition, wellness, and healthy lifestyle choices.
suitableFor: Students interested in food, nutrition, wellness, and making healthier lifestyle decisions.

name: Minor Ailments and Preventive Health
description: Minor Ailments and Preventive health aims to equip students with a comprehensive understanding of the principles to effectively manage minor ailments conditions that are prevalent in the community, including acute respiratory, gastrointestinal and dermatologicalΓö¼├íconditions. Students will also learn about therapeutic strategies encompassing pharmacological, non-pharmacological approaches, as well as lifestyle and behavioral interventions, to addressΓö¼├íHypertension, Diabetes and Hyperlipidemia. Students will gain insights on preventive measures and health promotion strategies to reduce the incidence of these common ailments, including immunizations and early detection programs. Through understanding the therapeutic principles behind drug use, students will be equipped with a foundation that can be applied in both healthcare and the pharmaceutical industry.
features: Covers hospitality, tourism, service operations, guest experience, and event contexts through real-world applications.
suitableFor: Students interested in hospitality, tourism, events, service design, and people-focused work.

name: Principles of Chemical and Biological Drug Action
description: Principles of Chemical and Biological Drug Action is designed to provide a comprehensive understanding of the fundamental principles underlying the actions of chemical and biological compounds used in the field of pharmacology and drug development. This module combines the principles of chemistry, biology, and pharmacology to explore how drugs interact with living organisms, target specific diseases, and produce therapeutic effects. Students will gain a deep understanding of the chemical and biological principles governing drug action, including drug-receptor interactions, pharmacokinetics, and pharmacodynamics. Students will also learn about the various mechanisms by which drugs exert their effects on the human body, such as enzyme inhibition, receptor activation, and signal transduction pathways. Concepts of drug metabolism, adverse effects, and the assessment of drug safety profiles will also be covered.
features: Covers biological concepts, cells, genetics, and scientific investigation through hands-on activities and project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in biology, life sciences, research, and scientific problem-solving.

name: Fundamentals of Pharmacy Practice
description: Students will gain the knowledge and skills required of a pharmacy technician to perform essential job functions in hospitals and community pharmacies. Through this module, students will be equipped with knowledge in pharmacy law, pharmacy inventory control and drug information resources. In addition, students will learn various uses of health supplements and over-the-counter products in managing common conditions. Students will also be introduced to mathematical formulas and medical abbreviations used in the industry.
features: Covers patient care, healthcare settings, communication, and professional practice through real-world applications.
suitableFor: Students interested in healthcare, patient support, clinical environments, and people-centred service.

name: Materials Science
description: This module aims to introduce students to the different types of manufacturing materials (metals, alloys, ceramics, polymers and semiconductors), with emphasis to the relationship between microstructure, properties, applications and performance of the materials. Students would appreciate materials science as a multidisciplinary subject with an integral part in serving the society and improving the lives of people.
features: Covers engineering concepts, technical systems, applied design, and practical problem-solving.
suitableFor: Students interested in engineering, technology, systems thinking, and hands-on technical work.

name: Project
description:
In this module, students will be equipped with skills to apply their domain knowledge through a team project to solve a real-world problem.

Students will apply the five steps in the project management process steps: initiating, planning, executing, monitoring and controlling, and closing to work on a team project. They will use Gantt charts and tools for budgeting and resource planning. There will be milestone checks incorporating supervisor input, with guidance for corrective action if necessary.

Students will also learn how to gather and organise data, prepare technical reports and make a presentation pitch to stakeholders. Students will be encouraged to adopt a multidisciplinary approach in the design of their project.
features: Covers laboratory practices, safety, documentation, quality standards, and compliance through hands-on activities and project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in laboratory work, applied science operations, quality control, and safe practice.

name: Biological Therapeutics
description: This module aims to introduce the common biological medicines derived from living systems. The overview of these biological molecules, mechanisms of action and its uses will be covered to enable student to better appreciate how these molecules can be applied in the treatment and management of various diseases. Key technologies in deriving these biological molecules will be covered, including its synthesis, manufacturing and characterization stages. Students will also acquire knowledge on the post-production analysis of these biological molecules in order to demonstrate understanding of these biological molecule properties, such as efficacy and stability.
features: Covers biological concepts, cells, genetics, and scientific investigation.
suitableFor: Students interested in biology, life sciences, research, and scientific problem-solving.

name: BIologics Production
description: This module delves into the development and manufacture of biologics or biopharmaceuticals and high value protein-based drugs that include monoclonal antibodies, growth factors using genetically modified expression hosts. Students will learn about upstream manufacturing science, which includes industrial bioreactor types, bioreactor design, cell inoculation, monitoring of growth parameters, shutdown and harvest of cultures, downstream processing operations like affinity and size exclusion chromatography and tangential flow filtration. Topics in this module will also focus on contamination control procedures and operations in a controlled clean room environment. Significant aspects of biologics quality assurance and control through implementation of current Good Manufacturing Practice (cGMP), Good Documentation Practice (GDP) and their impact on product quality and safety will also be covered.
features: Covers sustainability, environmental practices, and social responsibility through hands-on activities and project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in sustainability, environmental issues, and community impact.

name: Food and Nutritional Sciences
description:
This module focuses on basic concepts in human nutrition and the chemical changes that occur during production, storage and consumption of food. Students will acquire knowledge on how various food components and properties play a significant role in creating wholesome foods that are nutritious and safe. They will also apply this information in the field of diet planning and food product development, to adequately meet the nutritional needs of people in specific groups such as those who are pregnant or suffering from chronic diseases. Topics covered in this module also include understanding the essential role of macro and micronutrients in human health, mechanisms of immune reaction in response to food, and how changes in these processes may lead to nutritional deficiencies.

A306 Food Science and Nutrition will be renamed to A306 Food and Nutritional Sciences with effect from AY2025 intake
features: Covers food, nutrition, wellness, and healthy lifestyle choices through project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in food, nutrition, wellness, and making healthier lifestyle decisions.

name: Food Processing and Packaging
description:
Relating to the principles of food safety and quality, this module aims to equip students with knowledge and skills in diverse food processing techniques, including pasteurisation, fermentation, freezing, high-pressure processing, and pulsed electric fields. The practical-based curriculum teaches how these methods transform raw materials into high-quality, nutritious products, including functional ingredients. A significant aspect of the module is understanding the role of packaging in maintaining food quality and extending shelf life. Students will explore the science and innovation in packaging, learning about material selection, barrier properties, and sustainable packaging solutions like active and intelligent packaging. The curriculum covers various packaging technologies such as modified atmosphere packaging, microwaveable packaging, and retorting, and the interactions between food and packaging materials. Overall, students gain a comprehensive understanding of the interplay between processing and packaging techniques, essential for extending shelf life, ensuring food safety, and meeting consumer demands.

[Module Synopsis for students in AY2024 intake and earlier] 
This module aims to provide students with a general understanding of the processes related to the development of food products, from raw materials, ingredients, processing, preserving and packaging to the distribution of safe food products. Students will be introduced to food rheology and fundamental engineering concepts of mass and energy balances relevant to food processing. It then focuses on the understanding and application of unit operations such as thermal sterilization, UHT, dehydration, freeze-drying, spray drying, and separation processes. Students will also learn the importance of packaging to maintain food quality and to extend its shelf life. Concepts on food packaging, labelling, and new technologies that may bring advantages to the sensory, nutritional and safety aspects of food products will also be discussed.
features: Covers food, nutrition, wellness, and healthy lifestyle choices through hands-on activities and project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in food, nutrition, wellness, and making healthier lifestyle decisions.

name: Environmental Management and Assessment
description: The module provides an overview of methods to sustainably manage our impact on the environment through the introduction of management systems and assessment tools. Students will acquire knowledge of environmental law and learn how to implement and audit an environmental management system according to ISO standards. Various assessment tools such as life cycle assessment, environmental impact assessment, and strategic environmental assessment will be covered in this module. Students will also be introduced to environmental valuation methods, eco-labelling and green building requirements used internationally and locally.
features: Covers sustainability, environmental practices, and social responsibility through hands-on activities.
suitableFor: Students interested in sustainability, environmental issues, and community impact.

name: Quality Assurance in Agricultural and Food Products
description:
This module hones the students╬ô├ç├û skills in analytical methods for assessing the quality of food materials, focusing on their physical, chemical, and microbiological aspects. Students will engage in experiments with various food materials and apply their understanding of microbiology in the context of food spoilage and foodborne pathogens. The curriculum highlights good hygiene practices in food, manufacturing, and agricultural settings. A significant part of the module is dedicated to food safety regulations, teaching students to plan and maintain Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP) systems and familiarizing them with ISO standards like ISO 22000:2018. Additionally, the module introduces Good Agricultural Practice (GAP) principles for safe and wholesome agricultural produce, along with the importance of traceability systems in food and agriculture. Ultimately, students will learn to conduct food safety checks and implement hygiene procedures, culminating in a national certification in Workforce Skills Qualification (WSQ) Food Safety Level 3.

[Module Synopsis for students in AY2024 intake and earlier] 
This module focuses on the principles of analytical procedures used to provide information about the physical, chemical and microbiological quality of food materials. Students will perform experiments with various food materials, analyse data, and report their results. Personal hygiene and food handling practice from food and agricultural facilities to manufacturing and retail operations will be covered. This module aims to reinforce students╬ô├ç├û understanding of microbiology in relation to food spoilage and food poisoning microorganisms. As food regulations play a huge role in ensuring food security, students will learn how to plan, implement and maintain a HACCP system, as well as acquire knowledge of the ISO╬ô├ç├ûs International Standards e.g. ISO 22000:2018. This module also aims to introduce elements of Good Agricultural Practice (GAP), which are essential for creating safe and wholesome agricultural produce for consumers.
features: Covers food, nutrition, wellness, and healthy lifestyle choices through laboratory practice and project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in food, nutrition, wellness, and making healthier lifestyle decisions.

name: Food Innovation and Sustainability
description:
This module provides an introduction to the commercialization of new products or ingredients in the food industry, through knowledge of intellectual property (IP), marketing strategies and advanced technologies. Through this module, students will attain knowledge of the characteristics of various food ingredients and additives, as well as the problems associated with the use of food additives. This provides an overview on factors contributing to the quality and value of food products. Students will gain a practical approach in new product development and recognize the importance of sustainable innovation and production management of food, including agricultural produce.

[Module Synopsis for students in AY2024 intake and earlier] 
This module introduces the commercialisation of intellectual property (IP) and technologies associated with the development of new product or ingredients in food industry. Through an understanding of food science and technology, students are required to ideate, design and develop food products that value add to the food manufacturing industry. Students will apply their technical knowledge obtained from relevant modules to formulate the food product. In the product development process, they will also acquire skills in design thinking and project planning. They will also recognise the importance of sustainable innovation and production management for food products.
features: Covers food, nutrition, wellness, and healthy lifestyle choices through hands-on activities and project-based work.
suitableFor: Students interested in food, nutrition, wellness, and making healthier lifestyle decisions.

name: Formulation Science and Technology
description: This module introduces students to the multidisciplinary formulation industry of coatings, cosmetics, personal and household care products. Students will acquire knowledge on modern formulation science and the principles of making finished products from a mixture of different individual raw materials. Students will gain practical skills needed to formulate and test various representative products from the relevant industrial applications.
features: Introduces key concepts and practical understanding in formulation science and technology through hands-on activities and real-world applications.
suitableFor: Students who want an introductory understanding of formulation science and technology.

name: Petrochemical Technology
description: Petrochemicals are products manufactured using petroleum or natural gas as raw materials. This module covers the chemistry, processes and applications of products, intermediates and derivatives from the building blocks of raw materials namely, olefins (ethylene, propylene and butadiene) and aromatics (benzene, toluene and xylenes). Students will be equipped with the necessary laboratory skills to test and evaluate the properties of the petrochemicals and correlate to its composition.
features: Covers chemical principles, laboratory techniques, reactions, and data interpretation through hands-on activities and laboratory practice.
suitableFor: Students interested in chemistry, applied science, laboratory work, and analytical thinking.

name: Aquaculture Farm Management and Operations
description:
This module provides an overview of both land and marine-based aquaculture systems and their associated technologies, both locally and globally. Taking a hands-on industry-related approach and focusing on the production of commercially important aquatic species, students will also learn about the basic business considerations of farm operations. The principles of sustainable development will be discussed through case studies related to aquaculture development.

A316 Sustainable Aquaculture will be renamed to A316 Aquaculture Farm Management and Operations with effect from AY2025 intake
